claim: Stroke patients with prior use of direct oral anticoagulants have a lower risk of in-hospital mortality than stroke patients with prior use of warfarin.

result.claim: Stroke patients with prior use of direct oral anticoagulants have a lower risk of in-hospital mortality than stroke patients with prior use of warfarin.
result.reasoning: The claim is supported by evidence from a large retrospective cohort study (Paper ID: 3898784). The study, titled "Association of Intracerebral Hemorrhage Among Patients Taking Non–Vitamin K Antagonist vs Vitamin K Antagonist Oral Anticoagulants With In-Hospital Mortality," explicitly states that "Compared with patients with prior use of warfarin, patients with prior use of NOACs had a lower risk of in-hospital mortality (ARD, −5.7% [97.5% CI, −7.3% to −4.2%]; AOR, 0.75 [97.5% CI, 0.69 to 0.81])." This finding is reiterated in multiple propositions extracted from the paper (prop_1608, prop_1612). The study included 141,311 patients, providing a substantial sample size. While the paper's year and citation count are unknown, which limits the assessment of its recency and impact, the direct and consistent findings within this single large study provide strong support for the claim. No contradictory evidence was found.

evidence.text: The difference in mortality between NOAC-treated patients and warfarin-treated patients was numerically greater among patients with prior use of dual antiplatelet agents (32.7% vs 47.1%; ARD, −15.0% [95.5% CI, −26.3% to −3.8%]) than among those taking these agents without prior antiplatelet therapy (26.4% vs 31.7%; ARD, −5.0% [97.5% CI, −6.8% to −3.2%])
evidence.source: Association of Intracerebral Hemorrhage Among Patients Taking Non–Vitamin K Antagonist vs Vitamin K Antagonist Oral Anticoagulants With In-Hospital Mortality

evidence.text: 4918 patients (3.5%) were taking non–vitamin K antagonist oral anticoagulants (NOACs) preceding intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH).
evidence.source: Association of Intracerebral Hemorrhage Among Patients Taking Non–Vitamin K Antagonist vs Vitamin K Antagonist Oral Anticoagulants With In-Hospital Mortality

evidence.text: Patients without established atherothrombosis but with risk factors only (n = 8073) had the lowest risk of subsequent ischemic events (9.1%; 95% CI, 8.3%-9.9%).
evidence.source: Comparative determinants of 4-year cardiovascular event rates in stable outpatients at risk of or with atherothrombosis.

evidence.text: Among patients with ICH, prior use of NOACs or warfarin was associated with higher in-hospital mortality compared with no OACs.
evidence.source: Association of Intracerebral Hemorrhage Among Patients Taking Non–Vitamin K Antagonist vs Vitamin K Antagonist Oral Anticoagulants With In-Hospital Mortality

evidence.text: The unadjusted in-hospital mortality rate was 32.6% for patients with prior use of warfarin.
evidence.source: Association of Intracerebral Hemorrhage Among Patients Taking Non–Vitamin K Antagonist vs Vitamin K Antagonist Oral Anticoagulants With In-Hospital Mortality

evidence.text: The adjusted risk difference (ARD) for in-hospital mortality was 3.3% [97.5% CI, 1.7% to 4.8%] higher among patients with prior use of NOACs compared to patients without prior use of OACs.
evidence.source: Association of Intracerebral Hemorrhage Among Patients Taking Non–Vitamin K Antagonist vs Vitamin K Antagonist Oral Anticoagulants With In-Hospital Mortality

evidence.text: The adjusted odds ratio (AOR) for in-hospital mortality was 1.21 [97.5% CI, 1.11-1.32] for patients with prior use of NOACs compared to patients without prior use of OACs.
evidence.source: Association of Intracerebral Hemorrhage Among Patients Taking Non–Vitamin K Antagonist vs Vitamin K Antagonist Oral Anticoagulants With In-Hospital Mortality

evidence.text: The adjusted risk difference (ARD) for in-hospital mortality was 9.0% [97.5% CI, 7.9% to 10.1%] higher among patients with prior use of warfarin compared to patients without prior use of OACs.
evidence.source: Association of Intracerebral Hemorrhage Among Patients Taking Non–Vitamin K Antagonist vs Vitamin K Antagonist Oral Anticoagulants With In-Hospital Mortality

evidence.text: Compared with patients with prior use of warfarin, patients with prior use of NOACs had a lower risk of in-hospital mortality (ARD, −5.7% [97.5% CI, −7.3% to −4.2%])
evidence.source: Association of Intracerebral Hemorrhage Among Patients Taking Non–Vitamin K Antagonist vs Vitamin K Antagonist Oral Anticoagulants With In-Hospital Mortality

evidence.text: 15 036 patients (10.6%) were taking warfarin preceding intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH).
evidence.source: Association of Intracerebral Hemorrhage Among Patients Taking Non–Vitamin K Antagonist vs Vitamin K Antagonist Oral Anticoagulants With In-Hospital Mortality

evidence.text: The adjusted odds ratio (AOR) for in-hospital mortality was 1.62 [97.5% CI, 1.53 to 1.71] for patients with prior use of warfarin compared to patients without prior use of OACs.
evidence.source: Association of Intracerebral Hemorrhage Among Patients Taking Non–Vitamin K Antagonist vs Vitamin K Antagonist Oral Anticoagulants With In-Hospital Mortality

evidence.text: The study aimed to determine whether previous use of antibiotics decreases the risk of developing a first-time acute myocardial infarction.
evidence.source: Antibiotics and risk of subsequent first-time acute myocardial infarction.

evidence.text: The median stroke severity score for patients with prior use of warfarin was 9.
evidence.source: Association of Intracerebral Hemorrhage Among Patients Taking Non–Vitamin K Antagonist vs Vitamin K Antagonist Oral Anticoagulants With In-Hospital Mortality

evidence.text: Patients with prior use of warfarin were older.
